questions.question: You do not need to come with us ... you wish to.
questions.explanation: 'Unless' means 'except if' or 'if not'. 'You do not need to come unless you wish to' means: come only if you want to. This is the correct conditional conjunction here.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The boy hit himself ... the wall.
questions.explanation: 'Hit against' means to strike a surface with force. 'He hit himself against the wall' is the correct idiomatic expression for striking a wall.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The rainstorm prevented me ... going home.
questions.explanation: The verb 'prevent' is followed by 'from' + gerund. 'Prevented me from going home' is the correct collocational structure.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: My mother ... the Honourable Minster of Defence some twenty years ago.
questions.explanation: 'Some twenty years ago' signals a past action. 'Taught' is the simple past tense of 'teach'. It is the only past tense option available.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: "Do your work ... I will punish you," shouted the teacher.
questions.explanation: 'Or' presents an alternative/consequence. 'Do your work or I will punish you' is a threat where the punishment follows if the work is not done. This is the imperative + consequence structure using 'or'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: I talked ... with my mother today.
questions.explanation: 'Briefly' means for a short time. 'I talked briefly with my mother today' indicates a short conversation. The other options are frequency adverbs that don't describe the manner of the conversation naturally in this context.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: My father doesn't like ... on his bread.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'butter' — the dairy spread put on bread. 'Batter' is a cooking mixture, 'barter' means to trade, 'buter' is a misspelling.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The dancer tied a black cloth around her ...
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'waist' — the middle part of the body. Options B and D are misspellings. Option A ('west') is a direction, not a body part.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Father advised us to avoid ... of food.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'wastage' — the act of wasting. It comes from 'waste' + '-age'. Options A, B, and D all contain spelling errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Katiba was ... to go back to school by her elder brother because she was too young to get married.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'persuaded'. It comes from 'persuade' + '-d'. Options A, B, and C all contain spelling errors in different parts of the word.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: My employer pays me ...
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'fortnightly' — meaning every two weeks (a fortnight). It is spelled f-o-r-t-n-i-g-h-t-l-y. Options A, B, and D all contain spelling errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: " When did you report back? Mwenda asked."
options.B: " When did you report back" Mwenda asked?
options.C: " When did you report back?" Mwenda asked.
options.D: When did you report back, Mwenda asked.

questions.explanation: Direct speech questions have the question mark inside the closing quotation mark. The reporting clause 'Mwenda asked' follows outside the quotes and ends with a full stop. Option C correctly places the question mark inside the quotes.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.
questions.explanation: The name is 'Musa' (singular). The possessive is formed by adding 's: Musa's. No comma is needed between 'story' and 'is'. Option D correctly shows 'Musa's story is heart-breaking.'

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: Mercury Venus Earth and Mars are names of planets.
options.B: Mercury, Venus, Earth and Mars, are names of planets.
options.C: Mercury, Venus, Earth and Mars are names of planets.
options.D: Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars are names of planets.

questions.explanation: When listing items, commas separate each item. In British English style (standard in Zambia), the Oxford comma before 'and' is not required. 'Mercury, Venus, Earth and Mars are names of planets' is correctly punctuated with commas between items but not before 'and'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: "Please tell me a joke Tom requested."
options.B: "Please tell me a joke!" Tom requested.
options.C: "Please tell me a joke? Tom requested. "
options.D: "Please tell me a joke, Tom requested."

questions.explanation: A request/exclamation is enclosed in quotation marks with the exclamation mark inside the closing quote. The reporting clause 'Tom requested' is outside the quotes and ends with a full stop. Option B is correctly punctuated.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: Chisha, mother said angrily. " It will not help if you lose your temper."
options.B: "Chisha!" Mother said angrily, " It will not help if you lose your temper."
options.C: "Chisha; mother said angrily, it will not help if you lose your temper."
options.D: "Chisha, mother said" angrily, It will not help if you lose your temper."

questions.explanation: The sentence has two parts of direct speech: 'Chisha!' (exclamation calling the name) and 'It will not help if you lose your temper.' Option B correctly encloses 'Chisha!' in quotes with an exclamation mark, then has the reporting clause, then the second part of speech in quotes.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The parents were thankful for the good work done by the teachers. This word thankful means ...
questions.explanation: 'Thankful' means feeling or expressing gratitude. 'Grateful' is the closest synonym. Being thankful is being grateful for something received.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Mary is as beautiful as her young sister Edinah. This sentence means that ...

options.A: both Mercy and her young sister Edinah are beautiful.
options.B: both Mercy and her young sister Edinah are not beautiful.
options.C: her young sister Edinah is more beautiful than Mercy.
options.D: Mercy is more beautiful than her young sister Edinah.

questions.explanation: 'As beautiful as' is an equality comparison, meaning both Mary and Edinah are equally beautiful. Option A correctly states that both are beautiful. (Note: The question says 'Mary' but options say 'Mercy' — this appears to be a printing inconsistency in the original paper; the meaning is the same.)

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The learners reported for lessons just in time. The sentence means ...

options.A: there was enough time before the lessons.
options.B: there was no time before the lessons.
options.C: learners were late for the lessons.
options.D: learners did not report for lessons.

questions.explanation: 'Just in time' means arriving at the last possible moment — neither early nor late. This implies there was no extra time before the lessons. Option B best captures this meaning.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: When John got up, mother had prepared breakfast. This sentence means that ...

options.A: mother prepared breakfast after John got up.
options.B: breakfast was not prepared by mother.
options.C: mother prepared breakfast before John got up.
options.D: John did not get up until breakfast was prepared.

questions.explanation: 'Had prepared' is past perfect, indicating the breakfast was ready before John woke up. The past perfect shows the action (preparing breakfast) happened before another past action (John getting up). Option C is correct.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: We were hoping to receive a cake for christmas but grandmother just brought us oranges from the orchard saying, "A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush." The underlined sentence means that it is ...

options.A: better to appreciate what you have seen than what you have not seen.
options.B: good to appreciate whatever you have all the time.
options.C: not good to appreciate what you have seen than what you have not seen.
options.D: not good to appreciate whatever you have at all.

questions.explanation: The proverb 'A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush' means it is better to appreciate what you already have (oranges in hand) than to wish for something you don't have (a cake). Option A correctly captures this meaning.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The cattle return home at dusk. The word dusk in the sentence means the time when ...
questions.explanation: 'Dusk' is the time of day when daylight fades and it becomes dark — the period just after sunset. 'Daylight is fading' correctly describes dusk.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Matthew should watch out, otherwise he will lose it. The word watch out means to keep ...
questions.explanation: 'Watch out' means to be careful or stay alert to avoid danger. 'Keep alert' is the closest meaning — being watchful and attentive.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Human beings are classified as mammals. The word classified means ...
questions.explanation: 'Classified' means arranged or placed into categories/groups. Human beings are 'grouped' as mammals. 'Grouped' is the most accurate synonym for 'classified' in this context.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: Liswani grew up in a Christian family. He never missed Sunday school. He has been a humble boy since his childhood. No wonder he is now a pastor.
options.B: Liswani grew up in a Christian family. No wonder he is now a pastor. He never missed Sunday school. He has been a humble boy since his childhood.
options.C: He has been a humble boy since his childhood. Liswani grew up in a Christian family. He never missed Sunday school. No wonder he is now a pastor.
options.D: He has been a humble boy since his childhood. No wonder he is now a pastor. He never missed Sunday school. Liswani grew up in a Christian family.

questions.explanation: The logical order introduces Liswani's background → his religious commitment → his character → the natural conclusion. Option A flows: Christian family → never missed Sunday school → humble boy → now a pastor.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: My uncle is a greedy person. This is not good because children can suffer from malnutrition. Whenever there is nshima with meat, he adds a lot of chilli in the relish so that we fail to eat. He says that children should eat vegetables.
options.B: My uncle is a greedy person. Whenever there is nshima with meat, he adds a lot of chilli in the relish so that we fail to eat. He says that children should eat vegetables. This is not good because children can suffer from malnutrition.
options.C: Whenever there is nshima with meat, he adds a lot of chilli in the relish so that we fail to eat. This is not good because children can suffer from malnutrition. My uncle is a greedy person. He says that children should eat vegetables.
options.D: Whenever there is nshima with meat, he adds a lot of chilli in the relish so that we fail to eat. He says that children should eat vegetables. My uncle is a greedy person. This is not good because children can suffer from malnutrition.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: introduce the uncle as greedy → describe his greedy action (adding chilli) → state his excuse → conclude with the harm. Option B correctly flows from introduction to action to excuse to consequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: Mr Mutanda never reported late for work. Every one saw his commitment towards work. The school gave him an award on Labour Day. He was very excited.
options.B: Mr Mutanda never reported late for work. Every one saw his commitment towards work. He was very excited. The school gave him an award on Labour Day.
options.C: The school gave him an award on Labour Day. Every one saw his commitment towards work. He was very excited. Mr Mutanda never reported late for work.
options.D: He was very excited. The school gave him an award on Labour Day. Every one saw his commitment towards work. Mr Mutanda never reported late for work.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: Mutanda never came late → everyone noticed his commitment → school rewarded him with an award → he was excited about the award. Option A follows this natural cause-and-effect sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: He could not believe it! As a result, his wife and children deserted him. Mr Tembo had a happy family. However, after losing his employment, he could not provide for his family.
options.B: Mr Tembo had a happy family. However, after losing his employment, he could not provide for his family. As a result, his wife and children deserted him. He could not believe it!
options.C: Mr Tembo had a happy family. As a result, his wife and children deserted him. He could not believe it! However, after losing his employment, he could not provide for his family.
options.D: As a result, his wife and children deserted him. Mr Tembo had a happy family. However, after losing his employment, he could not provide for his family. He could not believe it!

questions.explanation: The logical order is: Mr Tembo had a happy family → he lost employment → could not provide → family deserted him → he could not believe it. Option B follows this correct chronological and causal sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: He ran away with it. He thought it was another dog with a piece of meat. So, he barked and unfortunately, the big piece of meat dropped into the water. One day, a dog found a big piece of meat in the house. As he was crossing a bridge, he looked into the deep water and saw a reflection of himself there.
options.B: One day, a dog found a big piece of meat in the house. He ran away with it. As he was crossing a bridge, he looked into the deep water and saw a reflection of himself there. He thought it was another dog with a piece of meat. So, he barked and unfortunately, the big piece of meat dropped into the water.
options.C: One day, a dog found a big piece of meat in the house. He ran away with it. He thought it was another dog with a piece of meat. So, he barked and unfortunately, the big piece of meat dropped into the water. As he was crossing a bridge, he looked into the deep water and saw a reflection of himself there.
options.D: One day, a dog found a big piece of meat in the house. As he was crossing a bridge, he looked into the deep water and saw a reflection of himself there. He thought it was another dog with a piece of meat. So, he barked and unfortunately, the big piece of meat dropped into the water. He ran away with it.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: dog finds meat → runs away with it → crosses a bridge → sees reflection → thinks it's another dog → barks → loses the meat. Option B follows this correct narrative sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: He got confused after getting his first salary. Mr Phiri was posted to a township school after completing his training. He did not plan how to spend his money hence, misused it. He ended up begging from friends.
options.B: Mr Phiri was posted to a township school after completing his training. He did not plan how to spend his money hence, misused it. He ended up begging from friends. He got confused after getting his first salary.
options.C: He did not plan how to spend his money hence, misused it. He ended up begging from friends. Mr Phiri was posted to a township school after completing his training. He got confused after getting his first salary.
options.D: Mr Phiri was posted to a township school after completing his training. He got confused after getting his first salary. He did not plan how to spend his money hence, misused it. He ended up begging from friends.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: Mr Phiri is posted to school → gets first salary and is confused → doesn't plan spending → misuses money → ends up begging. Option D follows this correct chronological and causal sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: At our home, we take supper early. After that, we all retire to bed. We then hold a family devotion to thank God for the day and pray for protection in the night. After eating, we watch television up to 20:00 hours.
options.B: At our home, we take supper early. We then hold a family devotion to thank God for the day and pray for protection in the night. After eating, we watch television up to 20:00 hours. After that, we all retire to bed.
options.C: At our home, we take supper early. After eating, we watch television up to 20:00 hours. We then hold a family devotion to thank God for the day and pray for protection in the night. After that, we all retire to bed.
options.D: At our home, we take supper early. After eating, we watch television up to 20:00 hours. After that, we all retire to bed. We then hold a family devotion to thank God for the day and pray for protection in the night.

questions.explanation: The logical evening routine is: take supper → watch TV after eating → hold family devotion → retire to bed. Option C follows this natural household sequence correctly.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: How many people suffered from cholera in Serenje District?
questions.explanation: From the bar graph, the bar for Serenje shows approximately 150 people affected by cholera in December 2014.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Which of the following are the symptoms of cholera?
questions.explanation: The classic symptoms of cholera are vomiting and diarrhoea (severe watery diarrhoea). This is a general knowledge question linked to the cholera graph context.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Which district had the least number of people suffering from cholera?
questions.explanation: From the bar graph, the bar for Lumbwa/Luano shows the lowest value at approximately 50 people. Among the options given, Luano has the smallest bar on the graph.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: What is the difference between the highest and lowest number of people who suffered from cholera?
questions.explanation: From the graph, the highest value is approximately 400 (Kapiri-Mposhi) and the lowest is approximately 50 (Lumbwa). The difference is 400 - 50 = 350.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: From the graph, we can conclude that many people suffered from cholera in the month of December because most ...

options.A: people drink rain water instead of purified water.
options.B: people do not boil their drinking water or add chlorine.

questions.explanation: Cholera spreads through contaminated water. The most logical conclusion from the December cholera outbreak is that water sources were contaminated. This is the primary cause of cholera epidemics.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The person who narrated this story was ...
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'When Sipho came to my class', 'I asked some children', 'I grabbed him and pushed him out of the classroom', 'I felt like a failure.' The narrator is clearly the class teacher.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Before he was allowed back into class, Sipho was made to ...
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'Sipho had to apologise to me before he was allowed back into my class.' 'Me' refers to the class teacher (the narrator). He apologised to the teacher.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: 'To grab someone' is to ...

options.C: get hold of him or her.

questions.explanation: 'To grab' means to take hold of someone suddenly and forcefully. 'Get hold of him or her' is the most accurate definition of 'grab'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: According to the passage, Sipho was ...
questions.explanation: Sipho ran away from the teacher laughing, ignored instructions, and continued running when counted to three. This behaviour shows he was not afraid of the teacher at all.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: When the class was sitting on the carpet ...

options.A: no one was allowed to talk.
options.B: some children were asked to talk.
options.C: only Sipho was allowed to talk.
options.D: everyone was allowed to talk except Sipho.

questions.explanation: The passage states: 'I asked some children in the class to tell me their news.' Some children were asked to share their news — not everyone, and not just Sipho.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: When people light their rooms at night, moths ...
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'When people light their rooms up at night, moths become a great nuisance as they happily swarm around the roof.' Moths become a nuisance when rooms are lit at night.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Why are moths not liked by most people? They look ...
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'Moths are not liked by many people because they look ugly.' The reason is explicitly stated — they look ugly.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: From the passage, we can tell that caterpillars are a source of ... food.
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'Caterpillars are a protein-giving food.' Protein is a body-building nutrient. 'Body building' food refers to protein-rich foods.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The word 'nuisance' in the passage has the same meaning as ...
questions.explanation: A 'nuisance' is something or someone that causes annoyance or inconvenience. 'Annoying' is the closest synonym for 'nuisance' in this context.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: "People who keep chickens invite them in to feed on the moths". In this sentence, the word 'them' refers to ...
questions.explanation: In the sentence 'People who keep chickens invite them in to feed on the moths', 'them' refers to the chickens. People invite the chickens in so that the chickens can eat the moths.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: This is the woman ... spoke about changes that occur during puberty.
questions.explanation: 'Who' is the relative pronoun for people when used as the subject of a relative clause. 'The woman who spoke' — 'who' refers to the woman and acts as the subject of 'spoke'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Nachamba and Chitalu are sex educators. ... teach about HIV and AIDS at school.
questions.explanation: 'Nachamba and Chitalu' is a plural subject (two people), so the correct pronoun is 'They'. 'He' and 'She' are singular, 'I' is first person.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Natasha did well in her examination because she ... attended all the lessons.
questions.explanation: Because Natasha did well, the reason must be positive — she 'always' attended all the lessons. 'Never', 'rarely', and 'sometimes' would not explain doing well in an examination.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: We found him being ... by unknown people.
questions.explanation: 'Being beaten' is the passive continuous construction (being + past participle). 'Beaten' is the correct past participle of 'beat' for the passive voice.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The games were put ... because of the rains.
questions.explanation: 'Put off' means to postpone or cancel. 'The games were put off because of the rains' means the games were postponed/cancelled. 'Put out' means to extinguish, which doesn't fit here.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The learners were blamed ... not sweeping the classroom in time.
questions.explanation: The verb 'blame' collocates with 'for'. 'Blamed for not sweeping' is the correct preposition usage — 'for' indicates the reason for the blame.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Namwinga smiled ... at the visitors.
questions.explanation: An adverb is needed to modify the verb 'smiled'. 'Cheerfully' is the adverb form. 'Cheerful' is an adjective. The sentence is not a comparison, so comparative/superlative forms are not needed.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: There is so ... dust in this room as well as outside.
questions.explanation: 'Much' is used with uncountable nouns. 'Dust' is an uncountable noun. 'So much dust' is the correct expression. 'Few' and 'many' are for countable nouns; 'plenty' requires 'of'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The football match between Italy and Zambia was watched by a lot of ...
questions.explanation: 'Spectators' are people who watch a sporting event. The correct noun for people watching a match is 'spectators'. 'Customers' are in shops, 'gangs' are criminal groups, 'tribes' are ethnic groups.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: I have to clean the house now, ... , I cannot go with you.
questions.explanation: 'Therefore' is a conjunctive adverb that introduces a conclusion or result. Because she has to clean the house, therefore she cannot go. This is a cause-and-effect relationship.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: ... men and women over there are waiting for a bus.
questions.explanation: 'Those' is the plural demonstrative pronoun for distant objects/people. 'Over there' signals distance, requiring 'those' (distant plural). 'These' is for nearby plural, 'That/This' are singular.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Either Kalumba ... Chikwakwa has collected my bag.
questions.explanation: 'Either ... or' is the correct correlative conjunction for a positive choice between two options. 'Either Kalumba or Chikwakwa' means one of the two has collected the bag.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: This book you are reading has been translated ... many languages.
questions.explanation: The correct collocation is 'translated into' a language. 'Translated into many languages' means converted from one language to many others. 'Into' indicates a change of form/state.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The teacher has given us an exercise to be ... at home.
questions.explanation: 'To be written' is the passive infinitive construction (to be + past participle). The exercise is to be written (by the students) at home. 'Written' is the correct past participle of 'write'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Nkisu ... a beautiful song last night at the concert.
questions.explanation: 'Last night' signals simple past tense. 'Sang' is the simple past tense of 'sing'. 'Sung' requires an auxiliary verb (has/had sung); 'sings' is present tense; 'singing' requires an auxiliary.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: You will not win this match ... you practice a lot.
questions.explanation: 'Unless' means 'if not'. 'You will not win unless you practice a lot' means: the only way to win is if you practice. 'Unless if' is redundant and not correct English.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: She spoke so ... at the meeting.
questions.explanation: 'Well' is the adverb form of 'good' and modifies the verb 'spoke'. 'She spoke so well' is grammatically correct. 'Good' and 'nice' are adjectives and cannot modify verbs.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Rape and defilement are the ... violations of human rights.
questions.explanation: 'The' before the blank and the context ('are the ... violations') signals the superlative is needed. 'Worst' is the superlative of 'bad'. 'Baddest' is not standard English.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The ... of worms swept through our field.
questions.explanation: The correct collective noun for worms (or army worms/caterpillars that move in large destructive groups) is 'army'. 'An army of worms swept through our field' is the correct expression. 'Swarm' is for bees/insects.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The time I visited Mpulungu Harbour, I saw a ... of ships.
questions.explanation: The correct collective noun for ships is 'fleet'. 'A fleet of ships' is the standard English expression. 'Bunch' is for flowers/fruit, 'heap' is for objects piled up.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A Zambian soldier has fled to a ... country.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'foreign'. It follows the 'ei' pattern after 'r': f-o-r-e-i-g-n. Options A, B, and D all contain letter-order errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The government declared Friday as a day of national ... for our late president.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'mourning' — a period of grief, especially after a death. 'Morning' is the time of day. Options C and D are misspellings.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Learners are encouraged to read more books in order to broaden their ...
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'vocabulary'. It is spelled v-o-c-a-b-u-l-a-r-y. Options A, B, and D all contain letter-order or substitution errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Her teaching was ... with photos taken during the festival.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'illustrated'. It has double 'l' at the start and ends in '-ated'. Options A, B, and C all contain spelling errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: We should ... adults who do not respect the privacy of boys and girls.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'doubt'. It has a silent 'b': d-o-u-b-t. Options A, C, and D all contain spelling errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: "Puberty is the change from childhood to adulthood," said Nkisu.
options.B: "Puberty is the change from childhood to adulthood, said Nkisu."
options.C: Puberty is the change from childhood to adulthood, "said Nkisu"
options.D: Puberty is the change from childhood to adulthood, said Nkisu.

questions.explanation: Direct speech is enclosed in quotation marks. The spoken words end with a comma before the closing quote, and the reporting clause 'said Nkisu' is outside the quotes followed by a full stop. Option A is correctly punctuated.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.
questions.explanation: A direct speech question has the question mark inside the closing quotation mark. The reporting clause 'Natasha asked' is outside the quotes followed by a full stop. Option B is correctly punctuated.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.
questions.explanation: This is a starting command, which ends with an exclamation mark. Commas separate the items in the command sequence. Option C correctly uses commas between 'marks', 'set', and ends with an exclamation mark inside the quotes.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: When mother was cooking in the kitchen, she asked Lungowe to give her salt, tomatoes, onions and green peppers.
options.B: When mother was cooking in the kitchen, she asked Lungowe, to give her salt, tomatoes, onions and green peppers.
options.C: When mother was cooking in the kitchen, she asked Lungowe "to give her salt, tomatoes, onions and green peppers."
options.D: When mother was cooking in the kitchen she asked Lungowe to give her salt tomatoes onions and green peppers.

questions.explanation: The sentence has an introductory adverbial clause followed by a comma. The items in the list (salt, tomatoes, onions and green peppers) are separated by commas. No comma is needed between 'Lungowe' and 'to'. Option A is correctly punctuated.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: He did not account for the missing money, did he?
options.B: He did not account for the missing money, does he?
options.C: He did not account for the missing money, didn't he?
options.D: He did not account for the missing money, doesn't he?

questions.explanation: This is a negative statement followed by a tag question. The statement uses 'did not', so the tag must use the positive auxiliary 'did' (not 'didn't'): 'did he?' A comma before the tag and a question mark at the end are required. Option A is correctly punctuated.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Brushing our teeth regularly is very important. The word regularly means ...
questions.explanation: 'Regularly' means consistently and at regular intervals — essentially 'always' doing something on a consistent schedule. This is the closest meaning among the options.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The teacher asked Martin why he was wearing a loose pair of trousers. The word loose in the sentence means Martin's pair of trousers were ...
questions.explanation: 'Loose' means not tight-fitting — there is too much room in the garment. 'Not fitting tightly' is the correct meaning of 'loose' clothing.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Neither Kabwe nor Kangwa is to blame. This sentence means that ...

options.B: none of them is to blame.

questions.explanation: 'Neither ... nor' is used for negative statements about two subjects. 'Neither Kabwe nor Kangwa is to blame' means not Kabwe and not Kangwa — none of them is to blame.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Our father asked us to set the ball rolling. The underlined part of the sentence means ...
questions.explanation: 'Set the ball rolling' is an idiom meaning to begin an activity or process. 'Get started' is the correct meaning of this idiomatic expression.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Neither Kabwita nor Likomba had their shirts stolen. This sentence means that ...

options.A: both of them had their shirts stolen.
options.D: none of them had their shirts stolen.

questions.explanation: 'Neither ... nor' with a negative action means neither person experienced it. Neither Kabwita's shirt was stolen nor Likomba's — so none of them had their shirts stolen.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Perfumes are highly flammable. The word flammable means ...
questions.explanation: 'Flammable' means easily set on fire — it catches fire easily. This is the standard definition of flammable/inflammable substances.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Mwansa's school bag is as light as a feather. This sentence means that Mwansa's school bag is ...
questions.explanation: 'As light as a feather' is a simile meaning extremely or very light in weight. 'Very light' is the correct interpretation. The bag is not literally a feather — it is being compared for lightness.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Emeldah was brought up by her uncle. The underlined words mean that the uncle ... Emeldah.
questions.explanation: 'Brought up' means raised or looked after a child. 'Cared for' is the closest meaning — the uncle raised and took care of Emeldah. 'Adopted' has a specific legal meaning not necessarily implied here.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: As the cattle were grazing, the two boys started playing with a bow and arrow and completely forgot about the cattle. One day, Mwansa and Mabvuto were herding cattle near the river. The cattle wandered off and went to drink water from a nearby river. As the cattle were drinking from the river, a crocodile appeared and reached out for one of them while the other two made desperate noises; as such, alerted the boys but it was too late. One cattle had been drowned and was being eaten by the crocodile.
options.B: One day, Mwansa and Mabvuto were herding cattle near the river. As the cattle were grazing, the two boys started playing with a bow and arrow and completely forgot about the cattle. The cattle wandered off and went to drink water from a nearby river. As the cattle were drinking from the river, a crocodile appeared and reached out for one of them while the other two made desperate noises; as such, alerted the boys but it was too late. One cattle had been drowned and was being eaten by the crocodile.
options.C: As the cattle were drinking from the river, a crocodile appeared and reached out for one of them while the other two made desperate noises; as such, alerted the boys but it was too late. One cattle had been drowned and was being eaten by the crocodile. The cattle wandered off and went to drink water from a nearby river. As the cattle were grazing, the two boys started playing with a bow and arrow and completely forgot about the cattle. One day, Mwansa and Mabvuto were herding cattle near the river.
options.D: The cattle wandered off and went to drink water from a nearby river. One day, Mwansa and Mabvuto were herding cattle near the river. As the cattle were grazing, the two boys started playing with a bow and arrow and completely forgot about the cattle. As the cattle were drinking from the river, a crocodile appeared and reached out for one of them while the other two made desperate noises; as such, alerted the boys but it was too late. One cattle had been drowned and was being eaten by the crocodile.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: introduce the setting (one day, herding cattle) → boys play and forget cattle → cattle wander to river → crocodile attacks → cattle drowned. Option B follows this correct chronological sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: Both have big game parks. Zambia has Luangwa and Kafue game parks while Malawi has Kasungu and Nyika game parks. Zambia and Malawi are neighbouring countries. These famous game parks attract many tourists both local and international.
options.B: Zambia and Malawi are neighbouring countries. Both have big game parks. Zambia has Luangwa and Kafue game parks while Malawi has Kasungu and Nyika game parks. These famous game parks attract many tourists both local and international.
options.C: Both have big game parks. Zambia and Malawi are neighbouring countries. Zambia has Luangwa and Kafue game parks while Malawi has Kasungu and Nyika game parks. These famous game parks attract many tourists both local and international.
options.D: Zambia and Malawi are neighbouring countries. Both have big game parks. These famous game parks attract many tourists both local and international. Zambia has Luangwa and Kafue game parks while Malawi has Kasungu and Nyika game parks.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: introduce the countries as neighbours → state both have game parks → name the specific parks → conclude with tourist attraction. Option B follows this natural informative sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: It is an idea or a piece of information passed on from one person to another. A message is a form of communication. These include letters, phones, smoke from fires, drums and radios. There are many different ways of sending a message.
options.B: A message is a form of communication. It is an idea or a piece of information passed on from one person to another. There are many different ways of sending a message. These include letters, phones, smoke from fires, drums and radios.
options.C: It is an idea or a piece of information passed on from one person to another. These include letters, phones, smoke from fires, drums and radios. There are many different ways of sending a message. A message is a form of communication.
options.D: A message is a form of communication. There are many different ways of sending a message. It is an idea or a piece of information passed on from one person to another. These include letters, phones, smoke from fires, drums and radios.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: define a message → explain what it is → state there are many ways → give examples. Option B follows this most logical expository sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: Water is a soluble substance. It is the centre of human activities. We use it in different ways such as drinking, washing, irrigation to generate power and many more. Indeed water is life.
options.B: Indeed water is life. It is the centre of human activities. We use it in different ways such as drinking, washing, irrigation to generate power and many more. Water is a soluble substance
options.C: Water is a soluble substance. Indeed water is life. It is the centre of human activities. We use it in different ways such as drinking, washing, irrigation to generate power and many more.
options.D: We use it in different ways such as drinking, washing, irrigation to generate power and many more. Indeed water is life. Water is a soluble substance. It is the centre of human activities.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: introduce water's property → describe its central role → give examples of uses → conclude with the main point. Option A flows: soluble substance → centre of activities → various uses → water is life.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: Mutale was unwell. He was taken to the hospital by his mother. The doctor diagnosed him with malaria. The doctor also advised him to sleep under a treated mosquito net. He was given assorted medicines.
options.B: The doctor diagnosed him with malaria. He was given assorted medicines. Mutale was unwell. The doctor also advised him to sleep under a treated mosquito net. He was taken to the hospital by his mother.
options.C: Mutale was unwell. He was taken to the hospital by his mother. The doctor diagnosed him with malaria. He was given assorted medicines. The doctor also advised him to sleep under a treated mosquito net.
options.D: The doctor diagnosed him with malaria. He was given assorted medicines. The doctor also advised him to sleep under a treated mosquito net. Mutale was unwell. He was taken to the hospital by his mother.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: Mutale is unwell → taken to hospital → doctor diagnoses malaria → given medicines → doctor advises mosquito net. Option C follows this correct medical-visit narrative sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: When the seeds are ripe, the bol bursts and the cotton wool fibres are then gathered. Cotton is a vegetable fibre which comes from a cotton plant. The cotton plant has a round, fluffy seed pod which is called a bol. The cotton plant flourishes in a warm climate but needs wet soil.
options.B: The cotton plant flourishes in a warm climate but needs wet soil. Cotton is a vegetable fibre which comes from a cotton plant. When the seeds are ripe, the bol bursts and the cotton wool fibres are then gathered. The cotton plant has a round, fluffy seed pod which is called a bol.
options.C: When the seeds are ripe, the bol bursts and the cotton wool fibres are then gathered. The cotton plant has a round, fluffy seed pod which is called a bol. The cotton plant flourishes in a warm climate but needs wet soil. Cotton is a vegetable fibre which comes from a cotton plant.
options.D: Cotton is a vegetable fibre which comes from a cotton plant. The cotton plant flourishes in a warm climate but needs wet soil. The cotton plant has a round, fluffy seed pod which is called a bol. When the seeds are ripe, the bol bursts and the cotton wool fibres are then gathered.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: define cotton → describe where it grows → describe the seed pod (bol) → describe what happens when seeds ripen. Option D follows this informative sequence from general to specific.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: Our planet is in trouble! Almost everyday, we hear of problems affecting the environment. Some of the major problems affecting the environment which contribute to climate change are pollution, deforestation and soil erosion. It is important that everyone does something to reduce further damage and destruction of the environment. Therefore, let us all campaign for a litter-free environment, stop cutting down trees and reduce air and water pollution as much as possible.
options.B: Our planet is in trouble! Therefore, let us all campaign for a litter-free environment, stop cutting down trees and reduce air and water pollution as much as possible. It is important that everyone does something to reduce further damage and destruction of the environment. Almost everyday, we hear of problems affecting the environment. Some of the major problems affecting the environment which contribute to climate change are pollution, deforestation and soil erosion.
options.C: Our planet is in trouble! Almost everyday, we hear of problems affecting the environment. It is important that everyone does something to reduce further damage and destruction of the environment. Some of the major problems affecting the environment which contribute to climate change are pollution, deforestation and soil erosion. Therefore, let us all campaign for a litter-free environment, stop cutting down trees and reduce air and water pollution as much as possible.
options.D: Our planet is in trouble! Therefore, let us all campaign for a litter-free environment, stop cutting down trees and reduce air and water pollution as much as possible. Almost everyday, we hear of problems affecting the environment. Some of the major problems affecting the environment which contribute to climate change are pollution, deforestation and soil erosion. It is important that everyone does something to reduce further damage and destruction of the environment.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: alert statement → describe the frequency of problems → name the specific problems → state importance of action → call to action. Option A follows this persuasive paragraph structure correctly.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: How much did the family spend on health services? They used ... hundred kwacha.
questions.explanation: From the budget table, Health expenditure = K200.00. Two hundred kwacha = 'two' hundred kwacha.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: What is the difference between the money used on rent and transport? It is ... kwacha.
questions.explanation: From the table: Rent = K700.00, Transport = K300.00. Difference = K700 - K300 = K400 = four hundred kwacha.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: What is the importance of a budget to a family? It helps the family to ...
questions.explanation: A budget is a financial plan that helps a family manage all aspects of their finances — income, expenditure, and savings. 'Plan well' is the most comprehensive answer that covers all aspects of budgeting.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: According to the passage, it is true that the money which the family received came from ...
questions.explanation: From the budget table, the Income section shows two sources: (i) Monthly salary and (ii) Family business. The family received money from two sources.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The word 'expenditure' in the text means money ...
questions.explanation: 'Expenditure' means money that is spent. From the table, the expenditure section lists all the items the family spent money on. 'Spent' is the most direct synonym.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Who are the best people to teach children about changes that occur at puberty?
questions.explanation: The passage states children 'often learn about the changes at puberty and sex from their peers' and this leads to 'myths that may confuse them and lead to risky behaviour.' The passage says 'it is important that children are taught about body changes' — implying by responsible adults, particularly parents, before changes occur.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: What would be the best title for this passage?
questions.explanation: The passage covers all aspects of puberty — physical changes in boys and girls, timing, emotional preparation, and risks. 'Puberty' is the most comprehensive title that encompasses all the content of the passage.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: According to the passage, physical changes at puberty occur at ... in boys and girls.
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'These changes differ from child to child. Some experience these changes as early as 9 years old, while in others, these may delay up to the age of 14.' Changes occur at different ages for different children.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: According to the passage, which of the following physical changes is common in both girls and boys?
questions.explanation: The passage mentions for girls: 'hips widen, pubic hair appears, breasts begin to develop and menstrual periods start.' For boys: 'sex organs, pubic hair and beards grow.' Pubic hair appears in both boys and girls — it is the only change common to both.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The word 'peers' in the passage means ...
questions.explanation: In the passage, 'peers' refers to other children of the same age from whom children learn about puberty. 'Friends' is the most appropriate synonym for 'peers' in this context (people of the same age/group).

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Which of the following diseases can one get by smoking cigarettes?
questions.explanation: Lung cancer is the disease most strongly and directly associated with smoking cigarettes. While smoking can contribute to other conditions, lung cancer is the most well-known disease caused by smoking tobacco.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Sipho was taken to the clinic by his ...
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'The teacher took Sipho to the clinic.' It was the teacher who took Sipho to the clinic, not a family member.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The nurse examined Sipho. According to this passage, what does the word examined mean?

options.A: Checking a person's body to find out what is wrong.
options.B: Giving a person a test to find out if he or she has learnt something.
options.C: Giving a person bed rest so that he or she can recover.
options.D: Giving a person some drugs so that he or she is treated.

questions.explanation: In the medical context of the passage, 'examined' means checking Sipho's body to determine what was wrong with him. The nurse examined him and found out he had smoked dagga — a physical assessment.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: After smoking, Sipho ...

options.A: went to the clinic to get examined.
options.B: started writing the work the teacher gave them.
options.C: started running around and shouting at other learners.
options.D: went home to sleep because he felt dizzy.

questions.explanation: The passage states: 'After a few minutes, Sipho started running around the school shouting and chasing other learners.' This happened immediately after smoking.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: What lesson can we learn from Sipho's behaviour after smoking dagga?

options.A: Smoking makes you know more about yourself.
options.B: Smoking has negative effects on an individual.
options.C: Smoking dagga makes you feel great about yourself.
options.D: Avoid peer pressure as it leads to bad behaviour.

questions.explanation: Sipho experienced coughing, running around uncontrollably, dizziness, and sickness after smoking. He vowed never to smoke again because it made him feel sick. The lesson is that smoking has negative effects on an individual.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: A policeman showed ... the way to the showgrounds.
questions.explanation: 'Us' is the object pronoun for 'we'. The policeman showed 'us' (object) the way. 'We' and 'I' are subject pronouns and cannot be used as objects; 'they' refers to other people not in the conversation.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Judith said that she was meeting ... friends in the afternoon.
questions.explanation: The subject is 'she' (Judith), so the possessive pronoun must be 'her'. 'Her friends' means the friends belonging to Judith. 'His' and 'their' refer to other parties.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: His father was angry ... him for losing the book.
questions.explanation: The correct collocation is 'angry with someone' (for being angry at a person). 'Angry with him' is the standard English expression when directing anger at a specific person.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The learners did not try to work ... hard.
questions.explanation: 'Very' is an adverb that intensifies the adjective/adverb 'hard'. 'Work very hard' is the correct expression. 'Much' is typically used with comparatives; 'more' requires a comparative context.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Nchimunya has been living in this town ... eight years.
questions.explanation: 'For' is used with the present perfect continuous to indicate the duration of time. 'Has been living for eight years' shows a period of time (how long). 'Since' requires a specific point in time.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Children must be very ... when crossing roads.
questions.explanation: After 'be very', an adjective is required (not an adverb). 'Children must be very careful' uses the adjective 'careful'. 'Carefully' is an adverb and cannot follow 'be'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: She ... decided to write her homework.
questions.explanation: An adverb is needed to modify the verb 'decided'. 'Quickly' is the correct adverb. The sentence is not a comparison, so comparative/superlative forms are not needed. 'Quick' is an adjective.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Moses was very tired last night ... he managed to finish his homework.
questions.explanation: 'But' shows contrast between two opposing ideas. Despite being very tired, he still managed to finish — these are contrasting ideas joined by 'but'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Mutinta and ... performed very well in English.
questions.explanation: When joined with 'and' as the subject of a sentence, the subject pronoun 'I' is used (not the object pronoun 'me'). 'Mutinta and I performed' is correct — both are subjects of the verb 'performed'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The old man ... asleep in his chair.
questions.explanation: The sentence describes a completed past action. 'Fell' is the simple past tense of 'fall'. 'Fallen' requires an auxiliary (had fallen); 'falling' requires an auxiliary (was falling).

questions.subject: English
questions.question: After he finished his work, he ... home.
questions.explanation: 'After he finished' sets the context in the past. 'Went' is the simple past tense of 'go', consistent with the past tense of 'finished'. 'Goes' is present tense; 'gone' requires an auxiliary.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: I have just ... making a fence.
questions.explanation: 'Have just' requires the past participle. 'Have just finished' is the present perfect tense (have + past participle). 'Finished' is the correct past participle of 'finish'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The ... was chanting slogans demanding the removal of their coach for being dishonest.
questions.explanation: The context refers to people who have a coach — this is a sports team. The collective noun for sports players is 'team'. The team was demanding the removal of their (sports) coach.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Kachepa is the ... learner in our class.
questions.explanation: 'The ... learner in our class' signals superlative degree (comparing to all others in the class). 'Most brilliant' is the correct superlative form of the adjective 'brilliant'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The ... cheered as the musician stepped on the stage.
questions.explanation: 'Audience' is the collective noun for people watching or listening to a performance. At a music concert, the people who cheer are the 'audience'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Mr Chintu has built a ... of flats near the shopping mall.
questions.explanation: A 'block of flats' is the correct collective noun/expression for a building containing multiple flats. 'Fleet' is for ships/vehicles, 'shoal' is for fish.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: I shall not give you the watch ... you have given me the money.
questions.explanation: 'Until' means up to the point when something happens. 'I shall not give you the watch until you have given me the money' means the watch will only be given after payment is made.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: ... the heavy rains, I managed to reach the church on time.
questions.explanation: 'Despite' is a preposition meaning in spite of, and is followed directly by a noun phrase. 'Despite the heavy rains' shows that the rains did not prevent reaching the church on time.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Last week's races were called ... because of heavy rains.
questions.explanation: 'Called off' is the phrasal verb meaning to cancel an event. 'The races were called off' means the races were cancelled. 'Called away' means summoned elsewhere.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Although their house is small, they live ... together.
questions.explanation: An adverb is needed to modify the verb 'live'. 'Happily' is the adverb form of 'happy'. 'Happy' is an adjective; 'happiness' is a noun; 'happiest' is a superlative adjective.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: It will be ... to watch the Zambia National Team play in the World Cup.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'exciting'. It comes from 'excite' + '-ing'. Options A, C, and D all contain letter duplication or transposition errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Inform your mother to reduce her ... for Bupe's birthday party.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'expenditure'. It starts with 'ex-' and ends in '-iture'. Options A, B, and D all contain spelling errors in the prefix or root.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Please contact my ... to make arrangements.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'secretary'. It is spelled s-e-c-r-e-t-a-r-y. Options A, C, and D all contain spelling errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: I want to see the house ... before I buy it.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'physically'. It comes from 'physical' + '-ly', with double 'l'. Options A, B, and D all contain spelling errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: She was the only one who ... the accident.
questions.explanation: The correctly spelled word is 'survived'. It is spelled s-u-r-v-i-v-e-d. Options A, B, and D all contain letter substitution or omission errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: Do you mind travelling a long distance?
options.B: Do you mind travelling a long distance.
options.C: Do you mind travelling a long distance,
options.D: Do you mind travelling a long distance!

questions.explanation: This is a question, so it must end with a question mark. 'Do you mind...' is an interrogative sentence. Option A correctly ends with a question mark.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: "Stand up, the captain," ordered the soldier.
options.B: "Stand up!" the captain ordered the soldier.
options.C: "Stand up the captain ordered the soldier!"
options.D: Stand up the captain, "ordered the soldier."

questions.explanation: Direct speech with a comma of address: 'Stand up, the captain,' — the comma separates the command from the direct address. The closing quote precedes the reporting clause. Option A is correctly punctuated.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: Even though the weather was stormy, Thandiwe felt excited about going to the party.
options.B: even though the weather was stormy, thandiwe felt excited about going to the party.
options.C: Even though the weather was stormy, thandiwe felt excited about going to the party.
options.D: even though the weather was stormy, Thandiwe felt excited about going to the party.

questions.explanation: A sentence must begin with a capital letter. The proper noun 'Thandiwe' must also be capitalised. A comma follows the introductory subordinate clause. Option A correctly capitalises the first word 'Even' and the proper noun 'Thandiwe'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: "Wait a minute," the nurse told me.
options.B: Wait a minute, "the nurse told me."
options.D: "Wait a minute, the nurse told me."

questions.explanation: Direct speech ends with a comma inside the closing quote mark, followed by the reporting clause outside. Option A correctly encloses 'Wait a minute,' in quotation marks with the comma inside, and the reporting clause 'the nurse told me.' follows outside.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the sentence which is correctly punctuated.

options.A: This car is theirs. Isn't it?
options.B: This car is theirs isn't it.
options.C: This car is theirs? isn't it.
options.D: This car is theirs, isn't it?

questions.explanation: A tag question requires a comma before the tag and a question mark at the end. 'This car is theirs, isn't it?' is the correctly punctuated tag question. The main clause ends before the comma, not with a full stop or question mark.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Chama is not only a cashier but also a farmer. This sentence means that Chama is ...

options.C: both a farmer and a cashier.
options.D: neither a cashier nor a farmer.

questions.explanation: 'Not only ... but also' means two things are true about the same person. Chama is both a cashier AND a farmer — simultaneously.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The sentence "If Peter had had money, he would have given it to John." means ...

options.B: John was given some money by Peter.
options.C: Peter didn't give John any money.
options.D: Peter gave some money to John.

questions.explanation: 'If Peter had had money, he would have given it to John' is a third conditional — it describes something that did NOT happen. Peter did not have money, therefore he did not give any to John.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The battery is draining very fast. The word draining means ...
questions.explanation: When a battery is 'draining', it is using up or finishing its energy/power. 'Finishing' is the closest meaning — the battery's charge is being used up and coming to an end.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The headteacher was upset when she found learners making noise in class. The word upset means ...
questions.explanation: 'Upset' means emotionally disturbed or unhappy. When someone is upset about something negative (noise in class), they are unhappy about it. 'Unhappy' is the most accurate synonym.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Neither the doctor nor the nurse saw the patient dying. This sentence means ...

options.A: both of them did not see the patient dying.
options.B: both of them saw the patient dying.
options.C: only the doctor saw the patient dying.
options.D: only the nurse saw the patient dying.

questions.explanation: 'Neither ... nor' with a negative action means neither person performed the action. Neither the doctor nor the nurse saw the patient dying — so both of them did not see it.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: It is sad that Tutu belongs to a gang now. The word gang means an organised group of ...
questions.explanation: A 'gang' is an organised group of criminals or people who engage in antisocial behaviour. The word 'sad' in the sentence confirms the negative connotation. 'Criminals' is the correct meaning.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Besides being a teacher, Mr Kainga is a volleyball coach. The sentence means that Mr Kainga ...

options.A: does nothing else but teach in school.
options.B: does nothing else but coach a volleyball team.
options.C: is a teacher and a volleyball coach at the same time.
options.D: was a teacher but now he coaches a volleyball team.

questions.explanation: 'Besides being a teacher' means in addition to teaching. Mr Kainga is simultaneously a teacher AND a volleyball coach — both roles at the same time.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Mr Brown was transferred to Nyimba Secondary School last year. The word transferred means ...
questions.explanation: 'Transferred' means officially moved from one place/post to another. Mr Brown was moved from his previous school to Nyimba Secondary School. 'Moved' is the closest synonym.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: Suwilanji shouted for help and ran away. She went back to the village without water. One early morning, Suwilanji decided to go to the river to draw some water. As she was walking, she saw a very big python.
options.B: She went back to the village without water. Suwilanji shouted for help and ran away. As she was walking, she saw a very big python. One early morning, Suwilanji decided to go to the river to draw some water.
options.C: One early morning, Suwilanji decided to go to the river to draw some water. As she was walking, she saw a very big python. Suwilanji shouted for help and ran away. She went back to the village without water.
options.D: As she was walking, she saw a very big python. Suwilanji shouted for help and ran away. One early morning, Suwilanji decided to go to the river to draw some water. She went back to the village without water.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: Suwilanji decides to go to the river → she sees a python while walking → she shouts for help and runs → she returns without water. Option C follows this correct chronological sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: When the results came out, she rushed to her son's school to get them. Her son had failed. However, because the boy had been well behaved and hardworking, he was offered a chance to repeat Grade 7. Mrs Chilinda was anxious to know how well her son had done in the Grade 7 Examinations.
options.B: Mrs Chilinda was anxious to know how well her son had done in the Grade 7 Examinations. When the results came out, she rushed to her son's school to get them. Her son had failed. However, because the boy had been well behaved and hardworking, he was offered a chance to repeat Grade 7.
options.C: Mrs Chilinda was anxious to know how well her son had done in the Grade 7 Examinations. Her son had failed. When the results came out, she rushed to her son's school to get them. However, because the boy had been well behaved and hardworking, he was offered a chance to repeat Grade 7.
options.D: Her son had failed. However, because the boy had been well behaved and hardworking, he was offered a chance to repeat Grade 7. Mrs Chilinda was anxious to know how well her son had done in the Grade 7 Examinations. When the results came out, she rushed to her son's school to get them.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: Mrs Chilinda is anxious → results come out and she rushes to school → her son failed → but because of good behaviour, he gets a second chance. Option B follows this correct narrative sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: One day, Juliet bought a packet of milk. She drank it hurriedly and threw the packet onto the street. She realised she had made a mistake. She then saw everyone looking at her.
options.B: One day, Juliet bought a packet of milk. She drank it hurriedly and threw the packet onto the street. She then saw everyone looking at her. She realised she had made a mistake.
options.C: She drank it hurriedly and threw the packet onto the street. One day, Juliet bought a packet of milk. She realised she had made a mistake. She then saw everyone looking at her.
options.D: She then saw everyone looking at her. She realised she had made a mistake. One day, Juliet bought a packet of milk. She drank it hurriedly and threw the packet onto the street.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: Juliet buys milk → drinks it and litters → sees everyone looking → realises her mistake. Option B correctly places 'seeing everyone looking' before 'realising the mistake' — seeing others' reactions triggers the realisation.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: Therefore, all the people must protect themselves from it. HIV is a virus that causes AIDS. This can then make a better Zambia for all of us. AIDS has no cure.
options.B: HIV is a virus that causes AIDS. Therefore, all the people must protect themselves from it. This can then make a better Zambia for all of us. AIDS has no cure.
options.C: HIV is a virus that causes AIDS. AIDS has no cure. Therefore, all the people must protect themselves from it. This can then make a better Zambia for all of us.
options.D: AIDS has no cure. Therefore, all the people must protect themselves from it. HIV is a virus that causes AIDS. This can then make a better Zambia for all of us.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: introduce HIV → state it has no cure → therefore people must protect themselves → this will make a better Zambia. Option C follows this correct informative and persuasive sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: After secondary school, he went to study medicine at the University of Zambia. Izenzi went to Edwin Mulongoti Primary School. He did his Grade 8 to 12 at Naboye Secondary School. He is now a doctor working in Switzerland.
options.B: He is now a doctor working in Switzerland. Izenzi went to Edwin Mulongoti Primary School. After secondary school, he went to study medicine at the University of Zambia. He did his Grade 8 to 12 at Naboye Secondary School.
options.C: Izenzi went to Edwin Mulongoti Primary School. After secondary school, he went to study medicine at the University of Zambia. He did his Grade 8 to 12 at Naboye Secondary School. He is now a doctor working in Switzerland.
options.D: Izenzi went to Edwin Mulongoti Primary School. He did his Grade 8 to 12 at Naboye Secondary School. After secondary school, he went to study medicine at the University of Zambia. He is now a doctor working in Switzerland.

questions.explanation: The logical chronological order of Izenzi's education is: primary school → secondary school (Grade 8-12) → university (after secondary school) → now a doctor. Option D follows this correct life-story sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: Lionesses do most of the hunting as they have to feed their cubs. Unlike other big cats, they often hunt together. Lions are flesh-eating animals. They usually creep up on their prey through the tall grass using their speed and strength.
options.B: Lions are flesh-eating animals. Unlike other big cats, they often hunt together. Lionesses do most of the hunting as they have to feed their cubs. They usually creep up on their prey through the tall grass using their speed and strength.
options.C: Unlike other big cats, they often hunt together. Lionesses do most of the hunting as they have to feed their cubs. They usually creep up on their prey through the tall grass using their speed and strength. Lions are flesh-eating animals.
options.D: Unlike other big cats, they often hunt together. Lions are flesh-eating animals. Lionesses do most of the hunting as they have to feed their cubs. They usually creep up on their prey through the tall grass using their speed and strength.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: introduce lions as flesh-eating → note their unique hunting style (together) → explain lionesses' role → describe their hunting technique. Option B flows: general fact → unique behaviour → specific role → hunting method.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Choose the paragraph which has the sentences in the best order.

options.A: One day, a boy fell sick. His mother decided to go and consult a witchdoctor about the sickness. She then decided to go and consult a medical doctor at the hospital and the boy was cured. The witchdoctor did not know how to cure the disease and her son's sickness worsened. She learnt a lesson from this life threatening situation.
options.B: One day, a boy fell sick. His mother decided to go and consult a witchdoctor about the sickness. The witchdoctor did not know how to cure the disease and her son's sickness worsened. She learnt a lesson from this life threatening situation. She then decided to go and consult a medical doctor at the hospital and the boy was cured.
options.C: One day, a boy fell sick. His mother decided to go and consult a witchdoctor about the sickness. The witchdoctor did not know how to cure the disease and her son's sickness worsened. She then decided to go and consult a medical doctor at the hospital and the boy was cured. She learnt a lesson from this life threatening situation.
options.D: One day, a boy fell sick. She then decided to go and consult a medical doctor at the hospital and the boy was cured. His mother decided to go and consult a witchdoctor about the sickness. The witchdoctor did not know how to cure the disease and her son's sickness worsened. She learnt a lesson from this life threatening situation.

questions.explanation: The logical order is: boy falls sick → mother consults witchdoctor → witchdoctor fails and condition worsens → mother consults medical doctor and boy is cured → she learns a lesson. Option C follows this correct narrative and causal sequence.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Which human right is respected when girls who fall pregnant are allowed to re-enter school? Right to ...
questions.explanation: Allowing pregnant girls to return to school respects their right to education. Education is a fundamental human right, and re-entry policies protect this right for girls who have given birth.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: What is the meaning of word re-enter? To ...
questions.explanation: The prefix 're-' means again. 'Re-enter' means to enter again — to come back to a place one has been before. 'Come back' is the most accurate meaning of re-entering school.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: This passage is mainly about ...
questions.explanation: The passage covers the invention of paper by the Chinese, its spread, the paper-making machine, writing surfaces before paper, and papyrus. The overarching topic is the invention and history of paper.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Paper was invented by the ...
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'The ancient Chinese invented paper about 2,000 years ago.' Paper was invented by the Chinese.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The machine that could make paper in a roll was discovered by ...

options.A: a Chinese scientist named Nicholas Robert.
options.B: a French man named Nicholas Robert.
options.D: an Egyptian man named Nicholas Robert.

questions.explanation: The passage states: 'a Frenchman named Nicholas Robert invented a paper-making machine that could make paper in continuous rolls.' He was French, not Chinese or Egyptian.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The word ancient in the passage means ...
questions.explanation: 'Ancient' means belonging to the very distant past — very old. In the passage, 'ancient Chinese' and 'ancient Egyptians' refer to people from long ago. 'Old' is the correct meaning.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Find the word in Paragraph 2 which has the same meaning as the word need.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states: 'the demand for paper increased.' 'Demand' means a strong need or requirement for something. It is the word in Paragraph 2 that has the same meaning as 'need'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: According to the passage, what is the other name for the lion?
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'a big lion, the King of the Forest decided that all old and powerless animals must be put to death.' The lion's other name/title is the King of the Forest.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: What would be the best title of this text?
questions.explanation: Kalulu (the old hare) is the hero of the story — he survived by his wits, hid in a cave, and devised a plan to save the lion. The story centres on Kalulu's cleverness. 'Clever Kalulu' is the best title.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: How did Kalulu survive the merciless killing of old animals? By ...
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'one clever old hare, called Kalulu, hid himself in a cave and survived this killing.' Kalulu survived by hiding in a cave.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: What lesson can be learnt from this passage?

options.A: Everyone is important in one way or the other.
options.C: It is bad to sleep with your mouth open.
options.D: It is good to save others.

questions.explanation: The lion realised that old animals are important after Kalulu saved him. He announced no old animal should be killed. The lesson is that everyone — even the old and seemingly useless — is important in one way or another.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The phrase to no avail in the story means ...
questions.explanation: 'To no avail' is an idiom meaning without success or without achieving the desired result. The animals tried to help the lion 'but to no avail' — meaning their efforts were unsuccessful.
